topics.title: Encapsulation & Access Modifiers in Java
topics.story: Imagine a car's engine. You don't reach into the engine to inject fuel directly — you press the gas pedal. The engine internals are hidden; you interact through a controlled interface. This is encapsulation: hide the data (engine parts), expose behavior (pedals, steering). In Java, a BankAccount stores a balance field, but you never set it directly. You call deposit(amount) and withdraw(amount). These methods enforce rules: no negative balance, no overdraft. The field is private — unreachable from outside — but the behavior is public. If the bank ever changes how interest is calculated, none of the calling code changes. The interface is stable; the implementation can evolve.
topics.analogy: A capsule (pill) is a perfect metaphor. The medicine inside is the data — critical, but you don't interact with it directly. The outer shell is the interface — you swallow the pill (call the method), the shell dissolves in the right place (implementation decides), and the medicine works (state changes). You don't need to know if the medicine is powder, liquid, or crystal. You just follow the interface: 'take with water.' Access modifiers are the walls: private = locked vault, package-private = office keycard, protected = family key, public = front door.
topics.memoryAnchor: PPPP ladder from NARROWEST to WIDEST — Private (this class only) < Package-private/default (no keyword, same package) < Protected (package + all subclasses anywhere) < Public (entire world). PIE rule for fields: Private fields + Interface (getters/setters) + Enforce invariants inside setters. Bank vault analogy: private=inside vault, package=employee floor, protected=all branches, public=ATM lobby. Golden rule: declare fields private by default, widen ONLY when forced. Never expose a mutable field as public — it breaks encapsulation completely.
topics.firstPrinciples: Encapsulation bundles data (fields) with the operations that act on that data (methods) and restricts direct access to the data. Java enforces this through access modifiers: private (same class only), package-private/default (same package), protected (same package + subclasses), public (everywhere). The principle is 'information hiding' — callers should not depend on implementation details. This reduces coupling: you can change the internal representation without breaking callers, add validation without changing the API, and maintain class invariants (e.g., age >= 0). Records (Java 16+) are immutable encapsulation: all fields are implicitly private final, and canonical getters are generated.
topics.bruteForce: Without encapsulation: all fields are public. account.balance -= 500; // direct mutation. No validation, no event hooks, no audit trail. Any class can set balance to -1,000,000. You cannot add logging because there's no single place to intercept. Changing the field name from 'balance' to 'currentBalance' breaks every class that used it. Coupling is at maximum.
topics.optimizedApproach: Fields private by default. Expose only what callers need via carefully designed methods. Prefer immutability (final fields, no setters) when the state shouldn't change. For mutable state, use setter-style methods with validation. Use the 'tell, don't ask' principle: instead of getBalance() + conditional logic in the caller, add a boolean canAfford(double amount) method to the account itself. Group related validation logic inside the class where the data lives.
topics.whenToUse: Always encapsulate. The only exceptions: simple data transfer objects (DTOs) where public fields are acceptable for brevity (records solve this elegantly), or constants (public static final). Increase visibility only when needed: start with private, expose protected if subclasses need it, public only for the designed API. Be especially strict with mutable collections — returning the internal list directly breaks encapsulation; return Collections.unmodifiableList() or a copy.

examples.title: Access Modifiers — The Four Levels
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"DECLARE","line":4,"variables":{"balance":"private"},"description":"private: only BankAccount methods can access balance"},{"phase":"DECLARE","line":5,"variables":{"transactionCount":"package-private"},"description":"No modifier = package-private: AuditService in same package can read it"},{"phase":"DECLARE","line":6,"variables":{"accountType":"protected"},"description":"protected: SavingsAccount subclass can access it"},{"phase":"DECLARE","line":7,"variables":{"accountId":"public final"},"description":"public + final: readable everywhere, never changeable"},{"phase":"CALL","line":21,"variables":{"amount":"500.0"},"description":"deposit(500): validates amount > 0, then balance += 500"},{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":23,"variables":{"balance":"1500.0","transactionCount":"1"},"description":"Balance updated; transactionCount incremented"},{"phase":"CALL","line":28,"variables":{"amount":"200.0"},"description":"withdraw(200): checks positive, under limit, sufficient funds"},{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":33,"variables":{"balance":"1300.0"},"description":"Balance decremented only after all validations pass"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[private balance] -->|getter only| B[public getBalance]
    A -->|validated write| C[public deposit]
    A -->|validated write| D[public withdraw]
    E[package-private transactionCount] -->|same package| F[AuditService.audit]
    G[protected accountType] -->|subclass| H[SavingsAccount.applyInterest]
    I[public accountId] -->|everywhere| J[Any class reads it]
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS BankAccount:
  PRIVATE balance          ← only this class
  PACKAGE transactionCount ← same package
  PROTECTED accountType    ← subclasses
  PUBLIC FINAL accountId   ← everyone

  deposit(amount):
    VALIDATE amount > 0
    balance += amount

  withdraw(amount):
    VALIDATE amount > 0, <= MAX, <= balance
    balance -= amount
examples.explanation: The four access levels create a privacy gradient. private is the most restrictive and should be the default for fields. Package-private (no modifier) is useful for helper classes in the same package. protected enables inheritance without full public exposure. public is reserved for the intentional API. The BankAccount enforces its invariant (balance >= 0) because balance is private — no external code can bypass the validation in deposit/withdraw.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's java.util.ArrayList uses private Object[] elementData and private int size. Callers use add(), remove(), get() — the internal array is never exposed. Spring's @Service beans encapsulate their repository dependencies as private fields injected via constructor. Hibernate entity fields are typically private with JPA @Column on getters or fields.

examples.title: Immutable Class — Defensive Copying
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"CALL","line":52,"variables":{"originalItems":"[Book]"},"description":"Create ImmutableOrder with items=[Book]"},{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":22,"variables":{"this.items":"List.copyOf([Book])"},"description":"Defensive copy: internal list is separate from originalItems"},{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":53,"variables":{"originalItems":"[Book, Pen]"},"description":"Caller mutates their list"},{"phase":"CALL","line":55,"variables":{"order.items":"[Book]"},"description":"getItems() still returns [Book] — internal copy unaffected"},{"phase":"CALL","line":57,"variables":{"order.total":"29.99"},"description":"withTotal(35.99): creates NEW ImmutableOrder"},{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":57,"variables":{"updated.total":"35.99","order.total":"29.99"},"description":"Original order unchanged; updated is a new object"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[new ImmutableOrder items] --> B[List.copyOf items]
    B --> C[Internal list sealed]
    D[originalItems.add Pen] --> E{affects internal list?}
    E -->|No| F[Internal still Book]
    G[withTotal newTotal] --> H[new ImmutableOrder]
    H --> I[Original unchanged]
    J[getItems] --> K[unmodifiableList wrapper]
examples.pseudocode:
FINAL class ImmutableOrder:
  ALL fields: private final

  constructor:
    VALIDATE inputs
    this.items = List.copyOf(items)  // defensive copy

  getItems(): return Collections.unmodifiableList(items)

  withTotal(newTotal): return NEW ImmutableOrder(same fields, newTotal)
  addItem(item):       return NEW ImmutableOrder(items+item, same rest)
examples.explanation: An immutable class requires: final class (no subclass can add mutability), all fields private final, no setters, validate in constructor, defensive copies for mutable fields both at construction (List.copyOf) and in getters (unmodifiableList). The 'with' pattern is how you 'change' immutable objects — you return new instances. Java's String, LocalDate, BigDecimal are all immutable. Records (Java 16+) automate this pattern.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's String is immutable — substring() returns a new String, not a modified one. This makes strings safe to use as HashMap keys and in concurrent code. Spring's @Value-injected configuration objects are often immutable records. Kafka message payloads should be immutable to prevent accidental mutation across consumer threads.

examples.title: JavaBeans vs Records — Encapsulation Styles
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"DECLARE","line":38,"variables":{},"description":"record PersonRecord auto-generates: private final fields, getters (no 'get' prefix), equals, hashCode, toString"},{"phase":"CALL","line":41,"variables":{"firstName":"Alice"},"description":"Compact constructor runs validation — no assignment needed, Java does it"},{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":65,"variables":{"rec":"PersonRecord[firstName=Alice, lastName=Smith, age=30]"},"description":"Record created — all fields private final"},{"phase":"CALL","line":66,"variables":{},"description":"rec.firstName() — accessor without 'get' prefix"},{"phase":"CALL","line":70,"variables":{"rec.equals(rec2)":"true"},"description":"Records compare by component values, not reference"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[PersonBean] -->|setXxx| B[Mutable state]
    A -->|getXxx| C[Read state]
    D[PersonRecord] -->|constructor| E[Immutable state]
    E -->|accessor| F[Read only]
    G[equals] -->|Bean: manual| H[Objects.equals each field]
    G -->|Record: auto| I[Component-wise comparison]
examples.pseudocode:
BEAN PersonBean:
  private fields
  getters + setters with validation
  manual equals/hashCode/toString

RECORD PersonRecord(firstName, lastName, age):
  compact constructor { validate only }
  auto-generated: getters, equals, hashCode, toString
  add custom methods as needed
  ALL fields immutable by default
examples.explanation: JavaBeans are mutable by design — required by older frameworks (JPA, JSP EL). Records are immutable data carriers — ideal for DTOs, value objects, and API responses. In a record, the compact constructor validates but doesn't assign — Java assigns after the compact constructor body. Record accessors use the field name directly (firstName()) not getFirstName(). Records work perfectly as HashMap keys since they're immutable and have proper equals/hashCode.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Boot 3.x supports records as @RequestBody, @ResponseBody, and even JPA projections. Kafka consumer records are immutable. Effective Java (3rd edition) recommends records for simple data aggregates. Jackson 2.12+ serializes/deserializes records automatically.

examples.title: Encapsulating Collections — Avoid Leaking Internal State
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":3,"variables":{"students":"public!"},"description":"BAD: public field — anyone can clear(), null, or replace it"},{"phase":"DECLARE","line":12,"variables":{"students":"private final"},"description":"GOOD: private final — cannot be replaced or nulled from outside"},{"phase":"CALL","line":22,"variables":{"student":"Alice"},"description":"enroll(): validates capacity and duplicate before adding"},{"phase":"CALL","line":33,"variables":{},"description":"getStudents(): returns unmodifiableList wrapper — same underlying list, but mutations throw"},{"phase":"CALL","line":63,"variables":{},"description":"view.add('Hacker') would throw UnsupportedOperationException"},{"phase":"RETURN","line":37,"variables":{},"description":"getStudentsCopy() returns new ArrayList — caller can sort/filter freely"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[BAD: public List] --> B[External: list.clear]
    A --> C[External: list = null]
    A --> D[External: list.add anything]
    E[GOOD: private final List] --> F[enroll: validated add]
    E --> G[drop: validated remove]
    E --> H[getStudents: unmodifiable view]
    E --> I[getStudentsCopy: safe copy]
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS StudentRoster:
  PRIVATE FINAL List<String> students

  enroll(student):
    IF full OR duplicate → return false
    students.add(student); return true

  getStudents():
    return unmodifiableList(students)  // view, no copy overhead

  getStudentsCopy():
    return new ArrayList(students)    // independent copy
examples.explanation: Returning a direct reference to an internal collection breaks encapsulation — callers can mutate it, bypassing all validation logic. Two strategies: unmodifiableList creates a view that throws on modification (lightweight, O(1)), or defensive copy creates an independent copy (safe for callers who want to sort/filter). The right choice depends on usage. The 'enroll' method enforces capacity and duplicate rules — these invariants would be impossible to maintain if the list were public.
examples.realWorldUse: java.util.Collections.unmodifiableList is used throughout the JDK. List.of() (Java 9+) returns an inherently immutable list. Spring's Environment.getPropertySources() returns an unmodifiable view. Hibernate's lazy-loaded collections must not be exposed directly — wrap them or return DTOs.

examples.title: Builder Pattern — Complex Object Construction
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"DECLARE","line":12,"variables":{},"description":"HttpRequest constructor is PRIVATE — only Builder.build() can create it"},{"phase":"CALL","line":72,"variables":{"method":"GET","url":"https://..."},"description":"Builder constructor: validates required fields"},{"phase":"CALL","line":74,"variables":{"header":"Authorization: Bearer token123"},"description":"header() adds to Builder's internal map, returns this for chaining"},{"phase":"CALL","line":77,"variables":{"timeoutMs":"3000"},"description":"timeout() updates Builder state"},{"phase":"CALL","line":78,"variables":{},"description":"build() validates then calls new HttpRequest(this)"},{"phase":"ASSIGN","line":13,"variables":{"headers":"unmodifiableMap copy"},"description":"HttpRequest takes defensive copy of headers map"},{"phase":"RETURN","line":78,"variables":{"req":"immutable HttpRequest"},"description":"Fully constructed, immutable HttpRequest returned"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Builder method url] --> B[.header]
    B --> C[.timeout]
    C --> D[.maxRetries]
    D --> E[.build]
    E --> F{Validate}
    F -->|OK| G[new HttpRequest Builder]
    G --> H[Private constructor copies all fields]
    H --> I[Immutable HttpRequest]
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS HttpRequest:
  ALL fields: private final
  PRIVATE constructor(Builder b)
  getters only

  STATIC class Builder:
    required: method, url
    optional: headers, body, timeout=5000, retries=3

    header(k,v) → this
    body(b)     → this
    build():
      validate
      return new HttpRequest(this)
examples.explanation: The Builder pattern solves the telescoping constructor problem (too many constructor overloads) while maintaining encapsulation and immutability of the product. The HttpRequest constructor is private — the only way to create one is through Builder.build(). The Builder validates required fields in its constructor and performs additional cross-field validation in build(). The product's headers map is defensively copied so callers cannot modify it after creation.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's StringBuilder, Stream.Builder, URI.Builder all follow this pattern. OkHttp's Request.Builder, Retrofit's Retrofit.Builder, Spring's RestClient.Builder and MockMvcRequestBuilders all use it. Lombok's @Builder generates the entire pattern automatically from field annotations.

problems.title: Fix the Broken BankAccount
problems.description: The given BankAccount class has all fields public and no validation. Refactor it so: balance is private with a validated deposit()/withdraw() (no negative amounts, no overdraft), owner is private with a non-null getter, and accountId is public final. Add canAfford(double) method. Preserve the same observable behavior for valid inputs.
problems.hint1: Change public fields to private. Add public getters where needed.
problems.hint2: deposit() validates amount > 0; withdraw() validates amount > 0 && amount <= balance.
problems.hint3: canAfford(amount) returns balance >= amount — don't expose balance directly for this logic.
problems.constraints: No public mutable fields allowed.
problems.editorial: Make all fields private. Add validated setters: deposit() checks amount > 0, withdraw() checks amount <= balance. canAfford() encapsulates the balance comparison — callers never access balance directly. toString() for debug output. The invariant (balance >= 0) is enforced at every entry point.

problems.title: Identify Access Modifier Levels
problems.description: Given a class in package com.app.model and a subclass in com.app.service and an unrelated class in com.other, predict which fields/methods are accessible from each location. Implement a method that returns which access level a member is from each context: 'accessible' or 'compile error'.
problems.hint1: private: same class only. Package-private: same package. protected: package + subclasses. public: all.
problems.hint2: The subclass in a DIFFERENT package can access protected members.
problems.hint3: Package-private is not accessible from a different package even in a subclass.
problems.constraints: Understanding based — no runtime reflection needed.
problems.editorial: private: class only. default (no modifier): same package. protected: same package + subclasses in any package. public: everywhere. The trap: protected is NOT accessible from a different package if you're not a subclass. Package-private is the most commonly misunderstood level.

problems.title: Temperature Class with Validation
problems.description: Create an immutable Temperature class with a value in Celsius. Add factory methods: celsius(double), fahrenheit(double), kelvin(double) — all convert to and store as Celsius internally. Provide toCelsius(), toFahrenheit(), toKelvin() getters. Validate: temperature cannot be below absolute zero (-273.15°C). Return a formatted string like '100.00°C / 212.00°F / 373.15K'.
problems.hint1: Store only Celsius internally. Factory methods convert before storing.
problems.hint2: F = C * 9/5 + 32. K = C + 273.15. Conversion formulas.
problems.hint3: Validate: if celsius < -273.15 throw IllegalArgumentException.
problems.constraints: Celsius = (F-32)*5/9. Kelvin = C+273.15. Precision: 2 decimal places.
problems.editorial: Factory methods (celsius, fahrenheit, kelvin) hide the constructor. Store everything as Celsius internally — convert on access. Validate in the factory method before constructing. Immutable: no setters, all fields final. toCelsius(), toFahrenheit(), toKelvin() compute on-the-fly from stored value.

problems.title: Counter — Thread-Safe Encapsulation
problems.description: Create a Counter class with increment(), decrement(), reset(), and getCount(). The counter cannot go below 0. Implement a getAndIncrement() that returns the value before incrementing. Add a threshold constructor: Counter(int threshold) — once count reaches threshold, increment() does nothing further. Demonstrate encapsulation prevents bypassing these rules.
problems.hint1: count is private. No setter for count — only controlled methods.
problems.hint2: decrement(): if count > 0, count--. Otherwise do nothing (or throw).
problems.hint3: threshold: if threshold > 0 && count >= threshold, increment() is a no-op.
problems.constraints: No synchronization needed. count starts at 0.
problems.editorial: Synchronized methods ensure atomicity of increment/decrement/reset. getCount() also synchronized for visibility. The floor is 0 — decrement does Math.max(0, count-1). Optional threshold: increment becomes no-op when count >= threshold. AtomicInteger is an alternative for lock-free thread safety.

problems.title: Encapsulated Stack
problems.description: Implement a generic Stack<T> with push(T), pop(), peek(), isEmpty(), size(). The internal storage is a private array or list. If pop/peek is called on an empty stack, throw EmptyStackException. Add a capacity constructor: if full, push throws StackOverflowException. Return the elements as an unmodifiable snapshot() without exposing the internal array.
problems.hint1: Use a private List<T> or private Object[] for storage.
problems.hint2: pop() removes and returns the last element; peek() returns without removing.
problems.hint3: snapshot() returns Collections.unmodifiableList(new ArrayList<>(storage)).
problems.constraints: Generic type T. snapshot() must not expose internal list.
problems.editorial: Back the stack with a private List<T>. push() calls list.add(). pop() removes last element after isEmpty() check — throw EmptyStackException. peek() returns last without removing. snapshot() returns an unmodifiable view so callers can inspect without modifying. size() delegates to list.size().

problems.title: Product Catalog — Defensive Copy on Return
problems.description: Implement a ProductCatalog that stores Product objects (id, name, price). The catalog's internal map must not be leakable. getProduct(id) returns an Optional<Product>. listAll() returns an unmodifiable list sorted by name. addProduct() validates that price > 0 and id is not null. removeProduct(id) returns true if removed. getSnapshot() returns a deep copy (new Product instances) of all products.
problems.hint1: Store as private Map<String, Product> catalog.
problems.hint2: listAll() returns new ArrayList from values(), sorted, wrapped in unmodifiableList.
problems.hint3: getSnapshot() creates new Product objects for each entry — a deep copy.
problems.constraints: Use Optional for nullable returns. listAll sorted by name.
problems.editorial: Return a deep copy: new Product(p.getId(), p.getName(), p.getPrice()) for each entry. Collections.unmodifiableMap() prevents structural changes but not mutation of mutable values — only a deep copy protects fully. getById() returns Optional<Product>.empty() for missing keys.

problems.title: Mutable vs Immutable String Chain
problems.description: Compare encapsulation in String (immutable) vs StringBuilder (mutable). Implement a StringChain class that wraps a StringBuilder but exposes only: append(String), prepend(String), length(), reverse(), and snapshot(). The underlying StringBuilder must not be accessible. Show that snapshot() returns an immutable String, not the builder itself. Also implement a reset() that clears the internal builder.
problems.hint1: private final StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder().
problems.hint2: prepend() uses sb.insert(0, str).
problems.hint3: snapshot() returns sb.toString() — a new String, inherently immutable.
problems.constraints: Fluent API — all mutation methods return this. StringBuilder must stay private.
problems.editorial: String is immutable — every operation returns a new String. StringBuilder is mutable — append() modifies in place, no new object. StringChain wraps StringBuilder privately. append() delegates to sb.append(), returns this for chaining. snapshot() returns sb.toString() — a new immutable String.

problems.title: Configuration Manager — Read-Only After Init
problems.description: Implement a ConfigManager that accepts key-value pairs during a loading phase, then can be locked. After lock(), any attempt to set() throws IllegalStateException. get(key) works always; getOrDefault(key, fallback) returns fallback if missing. Add getRequired(key) that throws NoSuchElementException if key is absent. All keys are case-insensitive. Return an unmodifiable snapshot as Map<String,String>.
problems.hint1: private boolean locked = false; set() checks !locked.
problems.hint2: Normalize keys to toLowerCase() in both set() and get().
problems.hint3: getSnapshot() returns Collections.unmodifiableMap(new HashMap<>(config)).
problems.constraints: Keys case-insensitive. Locked = no further writes.
problems.editorial: Use a boolean locked flag. All mutating methods check: if (locked) throw IllegalStateException. get() works after lock. lock() is idempotent — call twice with no error. getSnapshot() returns Collections.unmodifiableMap(new HashMap<>(config)) — a snapshot that reflects state at time of call.

problems.title: Person Record with Custom Accessors
problems.description: Create a PersonRecord with: firstName, lastName, age, email. Use compact constructor to validate: firstName/lastName non-blank, age 0-150, email contains '@'. Add computed methods: fullName() (first + last), initials() (first letter of each), ageGroup() (returns 'minor'/<18, 'adult'/18-64, 'senior'/65+), and maskedEmail() (shows only domain part: ***@gmail.com). Verify equals works by value.
problems.hint1: Compact constructor validates all fields — Java assigns them after the body.
problems.hint2: initials(): firstName.charAt(0) + '.' + lastName.charAt(0) + '.'
problems.hint3: maskedEmail(): split on '@', return '***@' + parts[1]
problems.constraints: Use record features. No mutable state.
problems.editorial: Record's compact constructor: validate firstName/lastName not blank (throw), age in [0,150] (throw), email contains '@'. fullName() = firstName + ' ' + lastName. maskedEmail(): split on '@', return '***@' + domain. Records are implicitly immutable — all fields final, no setters.

problems.title: Encapsulated Priority Queue
problems.description: Implement a BoundedPriorityQueue<T> where T implements Comparable<T>. It stores at most `capacity` items. offer(T item) adds if not full or if item is higher priority than the minimum (evicts minimum); poll() removes and returns highest priority item; peek() returns highest without removing; isFull() and size() are read-only. The internal heap/collection must not be accessible.
problems.hint1: Use a private PriorityQueue<T> internally with natural ordering.
problems.hint2: For bounded behavior: if size == capacity and newItem > peek (min), poll() then offer().
problems.hint3: Java's PriorityQueue is a min-heap — peek() returns smallest. For max-priority, use Comparator.reverseOrder().
problems.constraints: Min-heap internally. offer() evicts minimum when full and new item is larger.
problems.editorial: Java's PriorityQueue is a min-heap. For max-priority-first, pass Comparator.reverseOrder() to constructor. offer(): if size < capacity, add normally. If size == capacity, peek the minimum — if new item is greater, poll minimum and add new item. size(), peek(), poll() delegate to the internal queue.

problems.title: Validate and Encapsulate User Registration
problems.description: Implement a UserRegistration class. Fields: username (3-20 chars, alphanumeric + underscore), password (min 8 chars, has uppercase + digit), email (contains @ and .), role (enum: USER/ADMIN/MODERATOR). Password must be stored as char[] not String (security). Add a clearPassword() that zeros the array. Provide isPasswordMatch(char[] attempt) without exposing the stored password.
problems.hint1: char[] for password: Arrays.fill(password, '\0') to clear.
problems.hint2: isPasswordMatch(): compare Arrays.equals, then clear attempt array.
problems.hint3: Validation regex for username: [a-zA-Z0-9_]{3,20}. For password: check length, uppercase, digit separately.
problems.constraints: Store password as char[], never as String. Clear on match attempt.
problems.editorial: Regex for username: [a-zA-Z0-9_]{3,20}. For password: check length >= 8, contains uppercase (matches .*[A-Z].*), contains digit (matches .*\d.*). Collect all validation errors into a List<String> and throw a single exception with all messages — fail-all pattern, not fail-fast.

problems.title: Event History — Append-Only Log
problems.description: Implement an EventLog that can only append events (never delete or modify them). Events have: timestamp (auto-set to now), type (String), data (String). getEvents() returns an unmodifiable view. getEventsByType(type) returns filtered list. getSince(Instant from) returns events after that time. The internal list must not be clearable or modifiable from outside. Add a count() and latestEvent() that returns Optional<Event>.
problems.hint1: private final List<Event> events = new ArrayList<>(). No clear, remove, or set exposed.
problems.hint2: getEvents() returns Collections.unmodifiableList(events).
problems.hint3: getSince() uses stream().filter(e -> e.timestamp().isAfter(from)).collect(toList()).
problems.constraints: No delete or clear. Timestamps are Instant.now() at append time.
problems.editorial: Store events in a private List<Event>. Events are records with auto-set timestamp. append() adds to list — no remove, no modify method exposed. getAll() returns Collections.unmodifiableList(). getSince(Instant) filters by timestamp. The append-only invariant is enforced by having no removal methods.

problems.title: Nested Class Access Levels
problems.description: Demonstrate the four types of nested classes and their access to the outer class. Create an outer class Engine with private int rpm. Show: (1) static nested class EngineSpec that reads a static constant; (2) inner class Diagnostics that reads and writes private rpm; (3) local class inside a method that captures a local variable; (4) anonymous class implementing an interface. Return which ones can access private rpm.
problems.hint1: Inner (non-static) class has implicit reference to outer — can access private fields.
problems.hint2: Static nested class has NO outer reference — cannot access instance fields.
problems.hint3: Local class captures effectively-final local variables from the enclosing method.
problems.constraints: All four nested class types demonstrated.
problems.editorial: Static nested class: no outer instance reference, can access outer's private statics. Inner class: holds implicit reference to outer instance, accesses all outer members. Local class: defined inside a method, captures effectively-final local vars. Anonymous class: inline, used for one-off implementations.

problems.title: Fluent Builder with Validation Groups
problems.description: Implement a sophisticated FlightBooking builder. Required fields: origin, destination, departureDate. Optional: returnDate (if set, must be after departureDate), passengerCount (1-9), seatClass (ECONOMY/BUSINESS/FIRST), meal preference (STANDARD/VEGETARIAN/VEGAN). build() performs cross-field validation: return flight requires returnDate, FIRST class cannot have more than 4 passengers, VEGETARIAN/VEGAN with FIRST class adds surcharge flag.
problems.hint1: Store all builder fields. In build(), run validations in order: required, cross-field.
problems.hint2: returnDate != null && returnDate.isBefore(departureDate) → throw exception.
problems.hint3: Use a boolean surchageApplied flag in the product, set during build().
problems.constraints: Cross-field validation in build(). Private constructor enforces builder-only creation.
problems.editorial: Separate required from optional fields. Required: set at construction or throw. Optional: set via builder methods. Validate in build(): throw IllegalArgumentException listing all missing required fields. Return the built object from build(). Mark builder fields with validation state flags.

problems.title: Domain Model — Rich Encapsulation
problems.description: Implement a LibraryBook domain model. Fields: isbn (immutable, format XXX-XXXXXXXXXX), title, author, totalCopies, availableCopies. Methods: checkout() decrements available (throw if 0), returnBook() increments (throw if > total), reserve() marks a reservation if no copies available. Track checkout history as private List<String> (patron IDs). getCheckoutHistory() returns unmodifiable. Compute: isAvailable(), waitlistSize().
problems.hint1: ISBN validation: matches("\\d{3}-\\d{10}").
problems.hint2: checkout(patronId): if available==0, throw. Else available--, add to history.
problems.hint3: returnBook(): available++; if available > totalCopies throw (data inconsistency).
problems.constraints: ISBN format: 3 digits, dash, 10 digits. totalCopies immutable.
problems.editorial: isbn must match format XXX-XXXXXXXXXX (regex). availableCopies cannot exceed totalCopies. borrow() checks availableCopies > 0 before decrementing. returnBook() checks availableCopies < totalCopies before incrementing — detects data inconsistency. All fields private final except availableCopies.

problems.title: Reflection vs Encapsulation
problems.description: Demonstrate that Java reflection can bypass private access, and explain why this is a design concern. Write a SafeClass with a private int secret = 42. Use reflection to read and modify it. Then demonstrate how to partially defend against this using SecurityManager patterns or sealed/final classes. Also show that records are slightly harder to break via reflection. Write test assertions showing both the breach and the defense.
problems.hint1: Field f = SafeClass.class.getDeclaredField('secret'); f.setAccessible(true); f.get(obj).
problems.hint2: To partially defend: make the class final (no subclasses), use a SecurityManager (deprecated Java 17), or use Java 17 modules (strong encapsulation).
problems.hint3: Records in Java still allow setAccessible but the field is truly final — set attempt throws IllegalAccessException.
problems.constraints: Educational — shows encapsulation limits. Real defense is Java modules.
problems.editorial: field.setAccessible(true) bypasses private. The encapsulation is broken at runtime. Records have truly final fields — setAccessible(true) + field.set() throws IllegalAccessException. Use --add-opens JVM flag to control accessibility in Java 9+ modules. Demonstrate both success and failure paths.

problems.title: Singleton with Double-Checked Locking
problems.description: Implement a thread-safe DatabaseConnectionPool singleton with encapsulated connection management. The singleton is lazy-initialized using double-checked locking with volatile. Expose only: getInstance(), getConnection() (returns a mock Connection), releaseConnection(conn), getActiveCount(), getPoolSize(). The pool itself (List<Connection>) must be private. Add a resetForTesting() package-private method for test teardown.
problems.hint1: private static volatile DatabaseConnectionPool instance; — volatile for visibility.
problems.hint2: Double-checked: if(instance==null){synchronized(class){if(instance==null){...}}}
problems.hint3: Connection pool: two lists — available (free) and active (in use). getConnection() moves from available to active.
problems.constraints: volatile + synchronized for thread safety. Pool size 10.
problems.editorial: Double-checked locking requires volatile on the instance field to prevent reordering. IODH (Initialization-on-Demand Holder) is simpler and preferred — inner Holder class loaded lazily, class init is thread-safe. Connection pool: two lists (available, in-use). getConnection() moves connection between lists.

problems.title: Invariant Enforcement — Graph with Validated Edges
problems.description: Implement a DirectedGraph<T> that maintains these invariants: no self-loops, no duplicate edges, all referenced nodes must exist before adding edges. Encapsulate the adjacency list completely. Expose: addNode(T), removeNode(T) (also removes all edges to/from it), addEdge(T from, T to), removeEdge(T from, T to), getNeighbors(T), hasPath(T from, T to) via BFS.
problems.hint1: private Map<T, Set<T>> adjacency = new HashMap<>(). Sets prevent duplicate edges.
problems.hint2: addEdge: check both nodes exist, check from != to, then adjacency.get(from).add(to).
problems.hint3: removeNode: adjacency.remove(node); also remove node from all other sets.

testCases.input: addEdge(X,Y) where X not in graph

problems.constraints: No self-loops. No duplicate edges (use Set). All nodes must exist before adding edges.
problems.editorial: addNode(): add to adjacency map. addEdge(): validate from != to (no self-loop), check both nodes exist (throw), check edge not already present. removeNode(): remove from map + remove from all other sets. edgeCount() sums set sizes / 2 for undirected, as-is for directed. DFS/BFS uses adjacency sets.

problems.title: Layered Validation with Builder Chain
problems.description: Build a MortgageApplication builder with these validation layers: (1) field-level: income > 0, loanAmount > 0, creditScore 300-850, term in {10,15,20,30} years; (2) ratio-level: loanAmount <= 10x income, monthly payment <= 43% of monthly income (DTI rule); (3) policy-level: if creditScore < 620, maximum loan is 200,000; if creditScore >= 750, apply 0.5% rate discount. Return a MortgageApplication with rateApplied and approved flags.
problems.hint1: Run all field-level validations first, collecting errors into a list.
problems.hint2: Monthly payment: loanAmount * (r*(1+r)^n) / ((1+r)^n - 1) where r=monthlyRate, n=months.
problems.hint3: If any errors: throw IllegalArgumentException with all error messages joined.
problems.constraints: DTI = monthly payment / (income/12). Collect all field errors before throwing.
problems.editorial: Collect all validation errors across all three layers before throwing. Layer 1 (field): income > 0, loanAmount > 0, creditScore 300-850. Layer 2 (business): loanAmount <= income * 5. Layer 3 (regulatory): creditScore >= 620 for loanAmount > 500000. Join all errors with newline in the exception message.

problems.title: Encapsulation Audit Tool
problems.description: Write a class that uses reflection to audit another class's encapsulation health. For a given Class<?>, compute: publicFieldCount (public non-static fields — encapsulation violation), privateFieldCount (good), hasNoArgConstructor (JavaBean requirement), hasFinalFields (immutability indicator), hasEqualsAndHashCode (both or neither), getterSetterRatio (setters/getters — high ratio = more mutable). Return an EncapsulationReport with these metrics and a score (0-100).
problems.hint1: getDeclaredFields() for all fields; getModifiers() to check Modifier.isPublic(), isStatic(), isFinal().
problems.hint2: getDeclaredMethods() for methods; filter by name startsWith 'get' vs 'set'.
problems.hint3: Score: -10 per public field, +10 per private field, +20 if hasFinalFields, +20 if hasEquals, -5 per setter.

testCases.input: audit(BankAccount.class) — all private, no public fields

problems.constraints: Use getDeclaredFields/Methods. Skip static fields. Score clamped 0-100.
problems.editorial: Use reflection: getDeclaredFields() for fields, getDeclaredMethods() for methods. Count public vs private fields. Check for 'set' prefix methods as setters. hasEquals: check if class declares equals(Object). Score formula: publicFieldCount * -10 + privateFieldCount * 10 + bonuses. Return the computed score.
